risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "As women provided consent, a member of the research team randomly allocated them to either the DA or control group, using the Bernoulli function in Excel." Comment: The study explicitly describes a random allocation method using a computerized function in Excel, which supports that a truly random sequence was generated.

Quote: "As women provided consent, a member of the research team randomly allocated them to either the DA or control group, using the Bernoulli function in Excel." Comment: This describes how allocation was generated/applied, but does not report any explicit concealment mechanism (e.g. central randomization, pharmacy control, or sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes) that would prevent the person enrolling participants from knowing the upcoming assignment in advance. Therefore allocation concealment is not sufficiently described.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "One hundred and eighty-eight women enrolled in the study and were randomly allocated to the intervention (DA) or no intervention (control) group... Once women in the intervention (DA) group had completed the baseline questionnaire, they were sent a link to the electronic version of the DA... All women in the intervention group were asked if they had read and understood the DA... Women in the control group were contacted and asked if they had any questions or feedback about the questionnaire or study topic." Comment: Participants and study personnel were not blinded: women would know whether they received the decision aid versus no intervention, and personnel delivered different follow-up procedures by group. The main outcomes (knowledge, decisional conflict, self-efficacy, anxiety/depression, decision certainty) were largely self-reported and plausibly influenced by awareness of allocation, so lack of blinding could affect outcomes.

Quote: "The pre- and post-intervention questionnaires were completed online via SurveyMonkey. Once women in the intervention (DA) group had completed the baseline questionnaire, they were sent a link to the electronic version of the DA... Women in the control group were contacted... Once follow-up contact had been made, women in both groups were sent a link to the post-intervention questionnaire via SurveyMonkey." Quote: "The DCS is a 16-item self-report questionnaire..." and "the DA was self-administered and relied upon self-report data." Comment: Outcomes were assessed by patient self-report, so the participant was the outcome assessor. Participants were not blinded because the intervention group received the decision aid and the control group received no intervention. The study included subjective self-reported outcomes (decisional conflict, self-efficacy, anxiety/depression, decision certainty), which could be influenced by knowledge of allocation. By the worst-outcome rule, this makes detection bias high risk overall.

Quote: "A total of 188 women consented to participate in the study. Forty-four (28 DA; 16 Control) participants did not complete pre or post questionnaire and after a number of efforts to contact them, were assumed to have withdrawn from the study. The final sample of 144 participants consisted of 78 women ... in the DA group, and 66 women ... in the control group." Comment: Post-randomization missing outcome data are reported for the analysed pre/post questionnaire outcomes. Overall attrition was 44/188 = 23.4%, and it was imbalanced between arms: 28 losses in DA vs 16 in control. Using the final analysed numbers implies approximately 106 randomized to DA and 82 to control, giving attrition of about 26.4% vs 19.5% (difference about 6.9 percentage points). This exceeds the usual concern threshold of >20% loss with >=5 percentage point imbalance. The analysis appears based on completers only (final sample of 144), with no robust missing-data handling reported. Reasons were limited to non-completion/assumed withdrawal after failed contact, so the reported magnitude and imbalance are sufficient to judge high risk.
